text: Hi, I am Dr. Arun Prasad, Senior Surgeon at Indraprastha Polo Hospitals. I do robotic surgery for bariatrics, GI and thoracic. Let me show you how the XI robot feels like. So firstly, I will show you the backside of the robot where we have the controls. So now here I am standing behind the robot and with my hands I can grip these clutches. So this is similar to the trolley which we push at the airport. So only when I press this, the robot will move forward or backward or it will turn to one side or to another side.

The robotic system initiates the docking process for upper abdominal surgery, moving forward and turning laterally to the patient's right side. The robot and instruments are positioned for surgery, with the ability to adjust movements in multiple directions. Upon completion, the system returns to its original stored position.

The robot returns to its original position. The surgeon demonstrates the ATMM trocar and a typical robotic instrument, showing how the instrument connects to the trocar. The instrument is inserted into the trocar, allowing for 360-degree movement, unlike human fingers. The trocar is docked by pressing it into place, covered with a sterile drape. The endoscope is installed for targeting, and robotic instructions are heard as the instrument is inserted.

The robotic arm is positioned and adjusted to its desired location. The surgeon demonstrates changing instruments by pressing a button to release the current instrument, inserting a new one, and observing the green light flash to indicate readiness. The instrument is pushed down until the light turns blue, signaling proper insertion. The robotic arm is shown moving in various directions, offering nine degrees of freedom, which aids in complex dissections. The surgeon grips the control to maneuver the robot around.

So, these are some of the benefits of the robot which we have just seen. Next, I will take you to the console. Now that we have seen the parts of the robot, I will show you how the console works. So, this is the robotic console where the surgeon sits and now there is a screen here which tells me how to log in. So I now look for my name and touch my name. So this takes it back to the position where I had set it. So it's like you know Mercedes where you set your own position. After that you can still control things by these controls which are here. Now this moves the, this moves this handle down, up. This moves this view box up and down. It also can tilt the view box to my choice. And finally I have the foot controls. So now this foot tray comes out, left foot controls, switching of instrument, camera, and clutch. Right foot will control coterie and coagulation of the right instrument, coterie coagulation of the left instrument. If I have got the stapler inside, then depending upon which side I have, it will also control the clamping and cutting of the staplers.
The surgeon demonstrates the finger controls on the robotic console, showing how wrist movements are translated into the movement of robotic arms and instruments inside the abdomen during surgery.
